articles.title: Metformin’s hidden brain pathway revealed after 60 years
articles.summary: A major discovery reveals that metformin works not just in the body, but in the brain. By switching off a key protein and activating specific neurons, the drug lowers blood sugar through a previously hidden pathway, opening new doors for diabetes treatment.
articles.content:
For over 60 years, metformin has been a first-line treatment for type 2 diabetes, yet scientists have not fully understood how it works. Researchers at Baylor College of Medicine, along with international collaborators, have now identified an unexpected factor behind the drug's effects: the brain. By uncovering a brain-based pathway involved in metformin's ability to lower blood sugar, the team has opened the door to more targeted and effective diabetes therapies. The findings were published in Science Advances.

"It's been widely accepted that metformin lowers blood glucose primarily by reducing glucose output in the liver. Other studies have found that it acts through the gut," said corresponding author Dr. Makoto Fukuda, associate professor of pediatrics -- nutrition at Baylor. "We looked into the brain as it is widely recognized as a key regulator of whole-body glucose metabolism. We investigated whether and how the brain contributes to the anti-diabetic effects of metformin."

Rap1 Protein and the Hypothalamus

The researchers focused on a small protein called Rap1, located in a brain region known as the ventromedial hypothalamus (VMH). They found that metformin's ability to reduce blood sugar at clinically relevant doses relies on suppressing Rap1 activity in this specific area of the brain.

To test this idea, the Fukuda lab used genetically engineered mice that lacked Rap1 in the VMH. These mice were placed on a high-fat diet to model type 2 diabetes. When treated with low doses of metformin, their blood sugar levels did not improve. In contrast, other diabetes treatments such as insulin and GLP-1 agonists remained effective.

Direct Brain Effects of Metformin

To further confirm the brain's role, researchers delivered very small amounts of metformin directly into the brains of diabetic mice. Even at doses thousands of times lower than those typically taken orally, the treatment led to a marked reduction in blood sugar levels.

"We also investigated which cells in the VMH were involved in mediating metformin's effects," Fukuda said. "We found that SF1 neurons are activated when metformin is introduced into the brain, suggesting they're directly involved in the drug's action."

Neuron Activation and Blood Sugar Control

Using brain tissue samples, the team measured the electrical activity of these neurons. Metformin increased activity in most of them, but only when Rap1 was present. In mice that lacked Rap1 in these neurons, the drug had no effect, demonstrating that Rap1 is required for metformin to activate these brain cells and regulate blood sugar.

"This discovery changes how we think about metformin," Fukuda said. "It's not just working in the liver or the gut, it's also acting in the brain. We found that while the liver and intestines need high concentrations of the drug to respond, the brain reacts to much lower levels."

Implications for Diabetes Treatment and Brain Health

Although most diabetes medications do not target the brain, this research shows that metformin has been influencing brain pathways all along. "These findings open the door to developing new diabetes treatments that directly target this pathway in the brain," Fukuda said. "In addition, metformin is known for other health benefits, such as slowing brain aging. We plan to investigate whether this same brain Rap1 signaling is responsible for other well-documented effects of the drug on the brain."

Other contributors to this work include Hsiao-Yun Lin, Weisheng Lu, Yanlin He, Yukiko Fu, Kentaro Kaneko, Peimeng Huang, Ana B De la Puente-Gomez, Chunmei Wang, Yongjie Yang, Feng Li and Yong Xu. The authors are affiliated with one or more of the following institutions: Baylor College of Medicine, Louisiana State University, Nagoya University -- Japan and Meiji University -- Japan.

This work was supported by grants from: National Institutes of Health (R01DK136627, R01DK121970, R01DK093587, R01DK101379, P30-DK079638, R01DK104901, R01DK126655), USDA/ARS (6250-51000-055), American Heart Association (14BGIA20460080, 15POST22500012) and American Diabetes Association (1-17-PDF-138). Further support was provided by the Uehara Memorial Foundation, Takeda Science Foundation, Japan Foundation for Applied Enzymology and the NMR and Drug Metabolism Core at Baylor College of Medicine.

articles.personality_title: Scientists discover how diabetes drug metformin works in the brain
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Metformin is a common medicine used for over 60 years to treat type 2 diabetes. Until now, scientists believed it worked mainly by affecting the liver and gut to lower blood sugar.

**What happened** – Researchers at Baylor College of Medicine found that metformin also acts in the brain. They discovered that the drug lowers blood sugar by turning off a protein called Rap1 in a brain area named the ventromedial hypothalamus (VMH). Experiments with mice showed that when Rap1 was missing in this brain region, metformin did not lower blood sugar. Giving tiny amounts of metformin directly to the brain also reduced blood sugar.

**Impact** – This is the first time a brain pathway has been linked to metformin’s effects. It shows that metformin works at much lower doses in the brain than in the liver or gut. The findings could help scientists create new diabetes treatments that target the brain more directly, possibly improving how well they control blood sugar.

**What's next step** – Researchers plan to study whether this brain pathway also explains other benefits of metformin, such as slowing brain aging. They hope this knowledge will lead to better medicines that use the brain’s role in managing diabetes and brain health.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Scientists have found that metformin lowers blood sugar by acting in the brain, opening new possibilities for diabetes treatment and brain health research.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les scientifiques découvrent comment le médicament contre le diabète metformine agit dans le cerveau
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – La metformine est un médicament courant utilisé depuis plus de 60 ans pour traiter le diabète de type 2. Jusqu’à présent, les scientifiques pensaient qu’elle agissait principalement sur le foie et les intestins pour réduire le taux de sucre dans le sang.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des chercheurs du Baylor College of Medicine ont découvert que la metformine agit aussi dans le cerveau. Ils ont trouvé que le médicament réduit le sucre dans le sang en désactivant une protéine appelée Rap1 dans une zone du cerveau appelée hypothalamus ventromédian (VMH). Des expériences sur des souris ont montré que lorsque Rap1 manquait dans cette région, la metformine n’abaissait pas le sucre sanguin. Donner de très petites doses de metformine directement dans le cerveau a aussi fait baisser le taux de sucre.

**Impact** – C’est la première fois qu’un chemin cérébral est lié aux effets de la metformine. Cela montre que la metformine agit à des doses beaucoup plus faibles dans le cerveau que dans le foie ou les intestins. Ces résultats pourraient aider à créer de nouveaux traitements du diabète ciblant plus directement le cerveau, ce qui pourrait mieux contrôler le sucre dans le sang.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs prévoient d’étudier si ce chemin cérébral explique aussi d’autres bienfaits de la metformine, comme ralentir le vieillissement du cerveau. Ils espèrent que ces connaissances mèneront à de meilleurs médicaments utilisant le rôle du cerveau pour gérer le diabète et la santé cérébrale.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Les scientifiques ont découvert que la metformine réduit le sucre dans le sang en agissant dans le cerveau, ouvrant de nouvelles possibilités pour le traitement du diabète et la recherche sur la santé du cerveau.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos descubren cómo la metformina para la diabetes actúa en el cerebro
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – La metformina es un medicamento común usado desde hace más de 60 años para tratar la diabetes tipo 2. Hasta ahora, los científicos creían que funcionaba principalmente afectando el hígado y el intestino para bajar el azúcar en la sangre.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores del Baylor College of Medicine encontraron que la metformina también actúa en el cerebro. Descubrieron que la medicina baja el azúcar al apagar una proteína llamada Rap1 en una zona del cerebro llamada hipotálamo ventromedial (VMH). Experimentos con ratones mostraron que cuando faltaba Rap1 en esa parte del cerebro, la metformina no bajaba el azúcar. Dar pequeñas dosis directamente al cerebro también redujo el azúcar.

**Impacto** – Es la primera vez que se relaciona una vía cerebral con los efectos de la metformina. Esto muestra que la metformina actúa con dosis mucho menores en el cerebro que en el hígado o intestino. Los hallazgos podrían ayudar a crear nuevos tratamientos para la diabetes que apunten más directamente al cerebro, mejorando el control del azúcar.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores planean estudiar si esta vía cerebral también explica otros beneficios de la metformina, como frenar el envejecimiento cerebral. Esperan que este conocimiento conduzca a mejores medicinas que usen el papel del cerebro para manejar la diabetes y la salud cerebral.

**Resumen en una frase** – Los científicos descubrieron que la metformina baja el azúcar actuando en el cerebro, abriendo nuevas oportunidades para el tratamiento de la diabetes y la investigación sobre salud cerebral.

articles.personality_title: New smart, tiny devices improve how medicines reach the body
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Scientists have been working to create smaller and smarter devices that can deliver medicine directly inside the body. These devices use new technology from biology, artificial intelligence (AI), electronics, and materials science to make treatments more precise and easier to control.

**What happened** – Recent research has led to the development of various miniaturized drug delivery devices. Examples include tiny patches that respond to blood sugar levels to release insulin, implantable chips controlled wirelessly to deliver drugs on demand, and microneedle patches that can deliver medicine painlessly through the skin. Some devices mimic natural systems, like tiny jets inspired by sea creatures, to deliver medicine to specific tissues. Others combine sensors and AI to adjust treatment automatically.

**Impact** – These new devices can improve how medicines work by delivering the right amount at the right time and place. This helps treat serious diseases like cancer, diabetes, and heart conditions more effectively. They also make it easier for patients to stick to their treatment plans, as some devices can work without daily effort from the patient. The technology reduces side effects by targeting medicine precisely and can provide faster responses to changes in a patient’s condition.

**What's next step** – Researchers will continue to improve these devices, making them smaller, more reliable, and able to treat more diseases. Future steps include combining these technologies with AI to create fully automatic systems that monitor health and deliver medicine without needing doctor visits. Clinical trials will test these devices in more patients to ensure safety and effectiveness before wider use.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Tiny, intelligent drug delivery devices are being developed to make treatments safer, more precise, and easier to manage for patients with serious illnesses.
articles.personality_title_fr: De nouveaux dispositifs intelligents et miniatures améliorent la distribution des médicaments
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Des scientifiques travaillent à créer des dispositifs plus petits et plus intelligents capables de délivrer des médicaments directement dans le corps. Ces dispositifs utilisent des technologies nouvelles issues de la biologie, de l’intelligence artificielle (IA), de l’électronique et des sciences des matériaux pour rendre les traitements plus précis et contrôlables.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des recherches récentes ont permis de développer divers dispositifs miniaturisés de distribution de médicaments. Par exemple, des patchs minuscules qui réagissent au taux de sucre dans le sang pour libérer de l’insuline, des puces implantables contrôlées sans fil pour délivrer des médicaments à la demande, et des patchs à micro-aiguilles qui administrent des médicaments sans douleur à travers la peau. Certains dispositifs imitent des systèmes naturels, comme de petits jets inspirés de créatures marines, pour cibler des tissus précis. D’autres combinent capteurs et IA pour ajuster automatiquement le traitement.

**Impact** – Ces nouveaux dispositifs améliorent l’efficacité des médicaments en délivrant la bonne dose au bon moment et au bon endroit. Cela aide à mieux traiter des maladies graves comme le cancer, le diabète et les affections cardiaques. Ils facilitent aussi l’adhésion des patients aux traitements, certains fonctionnant sans effort quotidien. La technologie réduit les effets secondaires en ciblant précisément les médicaments et permet des réponses plus rapides aux changements de l’état de santé.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs vont continuer à améliorer ces dispositifs, en les rendant plus petits, plus fiables et capables de traiter davantage de maladies. Les prochaines étapes incluent la combinaison de ces technologies avec l’IA pour créer des systèmes entièrement automatiques qui surveillent la santé et délivrent les médicaments sans visite médicale. Des essais cliniques testeront ces dispositifs chez plus de patients pour garantir leur sécurité et leur efficacité avant une utilisation plus large.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Des dispositifs de distribution de médicaments miniatures et intelligents sont en cours de développement pour rendre les traitements plus sûrs, précis et faciles à gérer pour les patients atteints de maladies graves.
articles.personality_title_es: Nuevos dispositivos inteligentes y pequeños mejoran la entrega de medicamentos
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Científicos están creando dispositivos cada vez más pequeños e inteligentes que pueden entregar medicamentos directamente dentro del cuerpo. Estos dispositivos usan nuevas tecnologías de biotecnología, inteligencia artificial (IA), electrónica y ciencia de materiales para hacer los tratamientos más precisos y fáciles de controlar.

**Qué pasó** – Investigaciones recientes han desarrollado varios dispositivos miniaturizados para la entrega de medicamentos. Por ejemplo, parches pequeños que responden al nivel de azúcar en la sangre para liberar insulina, microchips implantables controlados de forma inalámbrica para administrar medicamentos según se necesite, y parches con microagujas que entregan medicamentos sin dolor a través de la piel. Algunos dispositivos imitan sistemas naturales, como pequeños chorros inspirados en animales marinos, para dirigir el medicamento a tejidos específicos. Otros combinan sensores e IA para ajustar el tratamiento automáticamente.

**Impacto** – Estos dispositivos nuevos mejoran cómo funcionan los medicamentos al entregar la dosis correcta en el momento y lugar adecuados. Esto ayuda a tratar enfermedades graves como cáncer, diabetes y problemas cardíacos de manera más efectiva. También facilitan que los pacientes sigan sus tratamientos, ya que algunos funcionan sin esfuerzo diario. La tecnología reduce los efectos secundarios al dirigir el medicamento con precisión y permite respuestas más rápidas a cambios en la salud del paciente.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores seguirán mejorando estos dispositivos, haciéndolos más pequeños, confiables y capaces de tratar más enfermedades. Los próximos pasos incluyen combinar estas tecnologías con IA para crear sistemas completamente automáticos que monitoreen la salud y entreguen medicamentos sin necesidad de visitas médicas. Se realizarán ensayos clínicos para probar estos dispositivos en más pacientes y asegurar su seguridad y eficacia antes de su uso generalizado.

**Resumen en una frase** – Se están desarrollando dispositivos inteligentes y diminutos para entregar medicamentos que hacen que los tratamientos sean más seguros, precisos y fáciles de manejar para pacientes con enfermedades graves.

articles.title: The brain's cleaning system can be boosted to rid Alzheimer's proteins
articles.summary: A duo of drugs that boosts our glympathic system, which clears waste from our brain, also improves the removal of proteins associated with the onset of Alzheimer's disease
articles.content:
A duo of drugs that boosts our glympathic system, which clears waste from our brain, also improves the removal of proteins associated with the onset of Alzheimer's disease

The glymphatic system is comprised of channels that clear waste from the brain CLAUS LUNAU/SCIENCE PHOTO LIBRARY

Drugs that boost our brain’s waste-disposal system so it can better remove proteins associated with Alzheimer’s disease have been identified for the first time. The combination of a therapy that is commonly used as a sedative with a medicine that prevents dangerously low blood pressure seems to safely and effectively remove proteins linked to the disease, which could delay its onset by seven years.

“This is a significant step forward,” says Shiju Gu at Harvard University, who wasn’t involved in the research. “It could benefit people with neurodegenerative disease, but even for healthy people, maybe you could use it to maximise the function of the brain.”

Our brain removes metabolic waste via the glymphatic system, a network of channels surrounding the blood vessels that pump waste fluid to the lymphatic system, where it is carried to the blood for disposal.

The glymphatic system is most active during deeper phases of sleep, when slow brain waves help push along waste fluid after it has been released from brain cells. But it becomes impaired with age, and especially during Alzheimer’s disease.

Researchers have previously found that dexmedetomidine, a drug commonly used as a sedative during medical procedures, boosts these brain waves in mice. It also improved the brain’s ability to clear waste fluid and slowed cognitive decline in mouse models of Alzheimer’s disease.

Free newsletter Sign up to Eight Weeks to a Healthier You Your science-backed guide to the easy habits that will help you sleep well, stress less, eat smarter and age better. Sign up to newsletter

To explore dexmedetomidine’s effects in people, Paul Dagum at pharmaceutical company Applied Cognition in Redwood City, California, and his colleagues recruited 19 adults – aged 60, on average – who were deprived of sleep for one night in a lab. The morning after, the participants – who had no chronic medical conditions or brain-specific issues – provided blood samples to act as baseline measurements.

They were then given 4 hours while they received an infusion of dexmedetomidine. They also took a drug called midodrine, which treats low blood pressure, a common side effect of dexmedetomidine. When they woke, the participants provided another blood sample.

A few weeks later, the researchers repeated the experiment, but this time, the participants had a placebo pill and saline infusions. The researchers then compared the two post-nap blood samples, accounting for variations in the two baseline samples.

This revealed that taking dexmedetomidine and midodrine, which the company collectively calls ACX-02, cleared two amyloid and tau proteins that are particularly prone to misfolding and forming clumps more effectively than the placebo/saline intervention.

The team estimates that if ACX-02’s effect were sustained over several years, it could delay the onset or worsening of Alzheimer’s disease by about seven years, based on levels of misfolded amyloid that are typically seen in people who go on to develop the condition, says Dagum. “That would be a significant, meaningful effect for those at risk,” adds team member Jeff Iliff at the University of Washington in Seattle.

Further analysis revealed that ACX-02 seems to work by boosting the number of slow brain waves during a phase of sleep in which the brain transitions from a light to a deeper stage. It also seems to increase the flow of fluid through the brain, so waste can be more quickly cleared. Finally, it appears to cause blood vessels to dilate and constrict more strongly, which pushes fluid more forcefully along glymphatic channels, says Iliff.

Antibody therapies that remove amyloid plaques, like lecanemab and donanemab, are approved in the US and UK, but they don’t have much of an impact on symptoms, and can cause brain bleeding and swelling. “There’s a huge need for new treatments – we know that the antibodies currently used for Alzheimer’s disease are not really working, and they can cause severe side effects,” says Natalie Beschorner at the German Centre for Neurodegenerative Diseases in Bonn.

No severe side effects were observed in Iliff’s trial. Boosting the glymphatic system doesn’t activate the brain’s immune cells in the way that antibody therapies do, which quickly raises the risk of side effects, says Dagum. ACX-02 also clears both amyloid-beta and tau. This may mean that its cognitive benefits are larger, says Dagum, adding that the team plans to verify this in studies involving people with early-stage Alzheimer’s disease.

Gu accepts that previous efforts to clear misfolded amyloid-beta and tau from the brains of people with Alzheimer’s disease have had limited benefits. Nevertheless, he adds that if the new approach is safe and effective, it could have a wide use. “It could benefit people with other brain conditions caused by a build-up of misfolded proteins, such as Parkinson’s,” he says. It might even be possible to develop a pill form of dexmedetomidine to treat attention lapses following sleep deprivation, says Iliff.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant medical breakthrough where a combination of existing drugs boosts the brain's glymphatic system to clear proteins linked to Alzheimer's disease, potentially delaying its onset by seven years. This has broad implications for neurodegenerative disease treatment and brain health, with a well-detailed scientific context and potential for large-scale positive impact on public health.
articles.personality_title: New drug combo helps brain clear Alzheimer's proteins, may delay disease by years
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – The brain has a cleaning system called the glymphatic system that removes waste, including harmful proteins linked to Alzheimer's disease. This system works best during deep sleep but slows down as people get older or develop Alzheimer’s.

**What happened** – Scientists tested two drugs together: dexmedetomidine, a sedative, and midodrine, which prevents low blood pressure. They gave this combination, called ACX-02, to 19 adults after a night without sleep. The drugs helped the brain clear away two harmful proteins connected to Alzheimer's better than a placebo.

**Impact** – This is the first time a drug combo has safely boosted the brain’s cleaning system in people. If used over several years, it could delay Alzheimer's symptoms by about seven years. Unlike current antibody treatments, this method doesn't cause serious side effects like brain swelling. It also works by increasing slow brain waves and fluid flow in the brain, improving waste removal.

**What's next step** – Researchers plan to test ACX-02 in people with early-stage Alzheimer’s to see if it improves thinking and memory. They also hope to explore if the drugs could help with other brain diseases involving protein build-up, like Parkinson’s.

**One-sentence takeaway** – A new drug combination boosts the brain’s natural cleaning system to clear Alzheimer's proteins, potentially delaying the disease by several years without serious side effects.
articles.personality_title_fr: Une nouvelle combinaison de médicaments aide le cerveau à éliminer les protéines de l’Alzheimer, retardant la maladie de plusieurs années
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Le cerveau possède un système de nettoyage appelé système glymphatique, qui élimine les déchets, y compris des protéines nuisibles liées à la maladie d’Alzheimer. Ce système fonctionne mieux pendant le sommeil profond, mais ralentit avec l’âge ou la maladie.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des scientifiques ont testé deux médicaments ensemble : la dexmédétomidine, un sédatif, et la midodrine, qui prévient l’hypotension. Ils ont donné cette combinaison, nommée ACX-02, à 19 adultes après une nuit sans sommeil. Les médicaments ont aidé le cerveau à éliminer deux protéines liées à Alzheimer mieux qu’un placebo.

**Impact** – C’est la première fois qu’une combinaison de médicaments stimule en toute sécurité le système de nettoyage du cerveau chez l’humain. Utilisée sur plusieurs années, elle pourrait retarder les symptômes d’Alzheimer d’environ sept ans. Contrairement aux traitements actuels par anticorps, cette méthode ne cause pas d’effets secondaires graves comme un gonflement du cerveau. Elle agit en augmentant les ondes cérébrales lentes et le flux de liquide dans le cerveau.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs prévoient de tester ACX-02 chez des personnes en début de maladie d’Alzheimer pour vérifier si cela améliore la mémoire et la réflexion. Ils espèrent aussi étudier son effet sur d’autres maladies cérébrales liées à l’accumulation de protéines, comme Parkinson.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Une nouvelle combinaison médicamenteuse stimule le système naturel de nettoyage du cerveau pour éliminer les protéines d’Alzheimer, retardant potentiellement la maladie de plusieurs années sans effets secondaires graves.
articles.personality_title_es: Nueva combinación de medicamentos ayuda al cerebro a eliminar proteínas del Alzheimer y puede retrasar la enfermedad años
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – El cerebro tiene un sistema de limpieza llamado sistema glinfático que elimina desechos, incluyendo proteínas dañinas relacionadas con el Alzheimer. Este sistema funciona mejor durante el sueño profundo, pero se vuelve menos eficiente con la edad o la enfermedad.

**Qué pasó** – Científicos probaron dos medicamentos juntos: dexmedetomidina, un sedante, y midodrina, que previene la presión arterial baja. Dieron esta combinación, llamada ACX-02, a 19 adultos después de una noche sin dormir. Los medicamentos ayudaron al cerebro a eliminar dos proteínas vinculadas al Alzheimer mejor que un placebo.

**Impacto** – Es la primera vez que una combinación de medicamentos mejora de forma segura el sistema de limpieza del cerebro en personas. Si se usa durante varios años, podría retrasar los síntomas del Alzheimer alrededor de siete años. A diferencia de los tratamientos con anticuerpos actuales, este método no causa efectos secundarios graves como hinchazón cerebral. Funciona aumentando las ondas cerebrales lentas y el flujo de líquido en el cerebro.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores planean probar ACX-02 en personas con Alzheimer en etapa temprana para ver si mejora el pensamiento y la memoria. También esperan estudiar si puede ayudar en otras enfermedades cerebrales con acumulación de proteínas, como el Parkinson.

**Conclusión en una frase** – Una nueva combinación de medicamentos potencia el sistema natural de limpieza del cerebro para eliminar proteínas del Alzheimer, retrasando la enfermedad varios años sin efectos secundarios graves.

articles.title: How working out like an astronaut can reduce back pain and slow ageing
articles.summary: The same principles that help astronauts stay strong in microgravity can help us all resist the slow collapse of ageing – and it’s not all about hitting the gym more
articles.content:
The same principles that help astronauts stay strong in microgravity can help us all resist the slow collapse of ageing – and it’s not all about hitting the gym more

cokada/Getty Images

During my years working with astronauts, it was routine to hear about returning crew being carried from their re-entry capsules on stretchers. Even last year, we saw this when NASA astronauts Suni Williams and Butch Wilmore finally came home after their unexpectedly long nine-month stint on the International Space Station.

Despite the top-notch health and fitness required to become an astronaut and the hours spent exercising while on missions, after several months in space, some astronauts can be more frail, less able to walk and more prone to injury than many older people back on Earth.

In fact, what an astronaut’s body goes through in orbit is, in some ways, like an accelerated preview of human ageing. The way that a space mission affects the spine, weakens muscles and detunes the balance system is a fast-forward version of what many of us experience when recovering from certain injuries, after a spell of hospital bed rest or simply due to years of sitting around.

This means that the way astronauts fight to maintain fitness during their missions, and then work to fully regain it when they return from space, holds crucial clues to healthier lives – and less back pain – for the rest of us. It also highlights the importance of the anti-gravity activities we should do each day if we want to stand up to the force that constantly tugs us down – and it’s not all about conventional gym workouts.

Humans have been going to space since 1961 and started occupying the International Space Station (ISS) in 2000. Since then, we have learned much about how a lack of gravity harms the body, especially the musculoskeletal system. Bones lose up to 2 per cent of their mass per month, with those that bear weight during walking losing the most, whereas arm bones are usually unaffected. Similarly, muscle strength can be reduced by up to 10 per cent in just a few weeks, rising to 20 per cent within three to six months.

Free newsletter Sign up to Eight Weeks to a Healthier You Your science-backed guide to the easy habits that will help you sleep well, stress less, eat smarter and age better. Sign up to newsletter

To counteract these effects, astronauts on the ISS now spend around 2 hours a day on exercise regimes, using a special treadmill, cycling or using a resistance machine designed for workouts in low gravity. But, for many, this still isn’t enough to compensate, and studies conducted on astronauts over the years have revealed more details about the impacts of microgravity.

One of the key lessons from space medicine is the importance of our “forgotten” core: the stabiliser muscles, situated deep behind our “six pack” abs, which keep the lumbar spine steady and braced within the abdomen. These include the multifidus, which lines each side of the spinal column and supports the movement of the vertebrae, and the transversus abdominis, a layer of muscle that wraps horizontally around our trunk like a corset.

Core muscle weakness

In the low gravity of space, many of the body’s muscles get smaller and weaker, and this is particularly true for postural muscles that keep us upright such as the multifidus and transversus abdominis. For instance, a 2021 study led by Julie Hides at Griffith University in Brisbane, Australia, looking at five astronauts who spent six months on the ISS found that the cross-sectional area of the multifidus in their lower back shrank by around 10 per cent, while their transversus abdominis shrank by 34 per cent.

In microgravity, the brain signals that activate these core muscles also become poorly timed, which means groups of fibres within the muscle aren’t fired up when needed for certain movements.

NASA astronaut Butch Wilmore is wheeled out of his return capsule after an unexpectedly long nine-month stint on the International Space Station NASA/Keegan Barber

Muscle atrophy contributes to the elongation of the spine seen in low gravity conditions. For instance, the torso of crew members can lengthen by over 6 centimetres – more than double the change typically experienced in our natural daily cycles on Earth.

The outcome of all this is that many astronauts experience back pain. A 2024 review found that more than half reported moderate to severe lower back pain during space flight, and some of those affected were still experiencing pain a year later.

So, keeping deep core muscles in good condition is now a key part not only of space medicine, but also of the rehabilitation of people with chronic back pain. However, these postural muscles don’t respond well to conventional weightlifting regimes typically used to build bigger muscles in our arms and legs, says Kirsty Lindsay at the Aerospace Medicine and Rehabilitation Laboratory at Northumbria University in Newcastle upon Tyne, UK. This means that even some well-trained sportspeople can have poor multifidus strength.

Spinal support

Instead, these spinal support muscles require a specific pattern of training in which they are working at a low level, almost continuously, says Lindsay – something that doesn’t happen in space. And, unlike with our abs or biceps, it is often hard to know when these muscles are “switched on” and contracting. This is why post-mission astronaut reconditioning programmes, like the one I was involved with at the European Astronaut Centre in Cologne, Germany, emphasise purposeful, controlled movement – what physiotherapists call motor control. In this, subjects learn how to activate muscles such as the multifidus and transversus, often using ultrasound to provide real-time biofeedback to show when they are contracted.

Once these activation skills have been mastered, the astronaut can then progress to other exercises, such as sitting to standing or stepping up and down, with gradually increasing loads and intensity and a focus on correct spinal posture. Such exercises are part of the post-flight reconditioning protocols of both NASA and the European Space Agency (ESA). Indeed, there are now devices like FRED (the Functional Re-adaptive Exercise Device, developed at Northumbria University) – a type of modified cross-training machine that provides little resistance – that have been designed to target the core stabiliser muscles and are suitable for people with frailty in physiotherapy clinics, as well as for deconditioned astronauts.

But new ways to exercise in space are also needed to maintain back health during missions. At Northumbria University, my colleagues are developing another method of training the deep core stabiliser muscles called low-intensity continuous activation (LICA) exercise. The idea is to enable these muscles to work in the correct way while in space and when astronauts return from their missions. When on the ground, the training involves making slow, controlled movements while the user’s balance is challenged, such as standing up on a stationary cycle-ergometer and slowly cycling with no resistance against which to work (there is also a walking version of this that can be replicated with machines like FRED). For use in space, however, new exercise devices will be needed.

LICA movements automatically recruit the core muscles, causing a low-level contraction. However, rather than simply flicking the muscles on and off – as happens with walking or weightlifting – LICA exercise keeps them activated throughout the movement, so the user doesn’t need to know where or how to contract the muscles.

Again, this development could benefit people on Earth, too, and tests show that LICA exercise can help with reconditioning following bed rest, as well as helping alleviate lower back pain and urinary stress incontinence following childbirth.

A number of “gravity-altering” systems can also help clinicians dial in appropriate levels of gravity for the people they are treating, to cue the core. After an injury, for instance, body-weight-support or anti-gravity treadmills can enable walking or running at 50 to 80 per cent of body weight, while trunk control is gradually re-established. These technologies were originally developed so that astronauts could practise walking on the moon and retrain for life under gravity after months in orbit.

Gravity-dosing technology

The most well-known of these devices is the Alter-G treadmill developed at NASA, which uses a sealed chamber enclosing the lower body in which increased air pressure lifts the runner. Studies show that such devices can reduce pain in people recovering from spinal, hip or knee surgery and can improve walking confidence in older people and those with neurological conditions.

Wearable technology, too, can offer assistance. Take the Gravity-Loading Countermeasure Skinsuit, an elasticated skintight suit mimicking the head-to-toe pull of gravity, which I led the development of at ESA for five years. The suit, designed for use in space, has been shown to reduce spinal elongation and back pain, while helping to keep the posture properly aligned and the deep stabiliser muscles working. This concept is now being spun into garments for people who remain firmly grounded on Earth, in the shape of clothes that support posture and trunk endurance – how long your core muscles can keep working without getting tired – in those with weak backs, chronic pain or age-related stoop.

However, leaving aside these high-tech measures, there are many simple anti-gravity habits we can all adopt. These include sitting for 10 minutes without a backrest; standing instead of sitting when taking a phone call; choosing the stairs instead of the lift; and, one of my favourites, standing on a train while loosely holding a rail or strap, forcing your body to make many tiny balance corrections. I started to do these activities regularly when I began to feel lower back pain after long working days, and experienced significant improvements. There is also some evidence that exercise regimes with a strong focus on building the strength of the core, such as Pilates, can help.

Simple challenges, like trying to keep your balance during a train journey, can help build the strength of the hidden muscles that support the spine GRANT ROONEY PREMIUM/Alamy

Time in space doesn’t just unmoor the body, it also confuses our balance system. In microgravity, the inner ear’s balance structures no longer respond to head tilts or gravity’s pull in the usual way. The sensory receptors in our muscles and joints that the brain relies on for proprioception – the sense of where our limbs are – are affected as well. Over time, the brain reduces its reliance on these cues and relies more on vision.

When astronauts come back to Earth, their time spent in low gravity leaves them unsteady and prone to overcorrecting or veering over when they walk, as their balance system recalibrates. NASA astronaut Tom Marshburn says that, 2 hours after returning from a space shuttle mission, he and his crewmate were laughing at the exaggerated steps they took when trying to walk up a ramp, lifting their feet far too high. “Also, when turning a corner in a hallway, I tended to miscalculate and stumble into the far wall.” One practical consequence is that astronauts aren’t allowed to drive for a week or two on return.

To restore coordination, space agencies prescribe what is known as sensorimotor reconditioning. I have watched astronauts working through eyes-closed balance tasks, some using wobble boards or carrying out exercises that combine the wearing of motion-tracking goggles with head-turn tasks to retrain the reflexes that link balance and vision.

What we see working for astronauts also benefits the rest of us. Indeed, honing and improving your balance is possible well into later life if you practise similar, progressively destabilising tasks, like standing on one leg while turning your head, walking heel to toe along a line or using balance boards or discs. For older adults, this kind of “neural tuning” can reduce the risk of falls and sharpen spatial awareness. In short: drills used for post-flight rehabilitation can help Earth-bound humans stay upright and independent.

Astronauts exercise for around 2 hours per day on the International Space Station, but this isn’t enough to counteract the negative impact of microgravity on the body NASA’s Johnson Space Center

Another bodily impact of time in space is the loss and weakening of bone. Healthy bones are the work of a dynamic process, a delicate dance of bone being broken down and built back up, known as bone reabsorption and formation. Microgravity uncouples these processes, which can cause bones to become more fragile and so more prone to fracture. One possible remedy for this is vibration. This was originally researched as a possible way to protect the bones and muscles of people on Earth, and evolved into the technique of whole-body, low-intensity vibration (LIV), which has now been extensively investigated as a way to preserve the bone health of astronauts.

Here, the individual stands on a machine that looks like a set of bathroom scales, which sends tiny vibrations up through their feet and into their legs, hips and lower spine. In doing so, stem cells found in bone marrow can be stimulated to turn into the cells that build bone back up. Now, short bouts of LIV are being clinically tested for use in treating osteoporosis and frailty, as well as in post-operative spine or hip rehabilitation, though firm evidence to support its use is pending.

Standing up to gravity

Perhaps none of this should be a surprise. Humans are bipedal creatures, highly adapted to spending much of our day upright in spite of gravity’s pull. Without this hidden kind of daily workout, our body quickly becomes undone. This is highlighted in studies of the impact of prolonged bed rest. Although the body still experiences gravity when we are lying down, it is no longer acting along our head-to-toe axis, and many of the consequences are similar to those seen in astronauts. Studies show that, just like in space, postural muscles become disproportionately weaker after prolonged bed rest. This is also why bed-rest studies, where volunteers lie down for months at a time, are used to investigate how the body adapts to weightlessness.

In the end, my years working in the field of space medicine have caused me to view gravity not as a simple constant, but as a training partner. The same principles that help astronauts stand tall after months in microgravity can help us all resist the slow collapse brought on by time and inactivity. Gravity, it turns out, is both the challenge that wears us down and the medicine that holds us up.

articles.personality_title: How astronaut exercise methods help reduce back pain and slow ageing
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Astronauts lose muscle strength and bone mass during long space missions because of microgravity, which is like an accelerated version of ageing on Earth. After returning, many astronauts struggle with back pain and balance problems.

**What happened** – Scientists studied astronauts’ bodies and discovered that special core muscles supporting the spine shrink and weaken in space. Space agencies now use targeted exercises and new devices to help astronauts keep these muscles strong during missions and recover after returning to Earth. Some of these methods, like low-intensity continuous activation (LICA) exercises and gravity-support suits, are being adapted to help people on Earth with back pain, balance issues, and muscle weakness.

**Impact** – This research shows that the way astronauts maintain muscle and bone health in space can also improve everyday health problems, such as chronic back pain and age-related muscle loss. Techniques like balance training and special low-resistance workouts strengthen muscles that normal gym exercises often miss. Anti-gravity devices originally made for space are now helping patients recover from injuries and older adults improve mobility.

**What's next step** – Scientists are developing better exercise machines for space and Earth to keep core muscles active continuously. New wearable suits and vibration therapies are being tested to protect bones and improve posture. People on Earth can also use simple habits like standing more, balance exercises, and core-focused workouts to support spine health and slow ageing effects.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Exercises and technologies designed for astronauts in space are helping people on Earth reduce back pain, improve balance, and maintain muscle strength as they age.
articles.personality_title_fr: Comment les exercices des astronautes réduisent le mal de dos et ralentissent le vieillissement
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les astronautes perdent de la force musculaire et de la masse osseuse lors de longues missions spatiales à cause de la microgravité, qui ressemble à une version accélérée du vieillissement sur Terre.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Les scientifiques ont découvert que certains muscles profonds qui soutiennent la colonne vertébrale s’atrophient dans l’espace. Les agences spatiales utilisent maintenant des exercices ciblés et des appareils innovants pour aider les astronautes à garder ces muscles forts pendant la mission et à se rétablir après leur retour. Ces méthodes, comme les exercices à activation continue à faible intensité (LICA) et les combinaisons de soutien gravitationnel, sont adaptées pour aider les personnes sur Terre souffrant de douleurs dorsales, de problèmes d’équilibre et de faiblesse musculaire.

**Impact** – Ces recherches montrent que les techniques utilisées pour maintenir la santé musculaire et osseuse dans l’espace peuvent aussi améliorer des problèmes de santé courants comme le mal de dos chronique et la perte musculaire liée à l’âge. Des entraînements spécifiques renforcent des muscles souvent oubliés par les exercices classiques. Des appareils anti-gravité créés pour l’espace aident désormais les patients en rééducation et les personnes âgées à mieux bouger.

**Prochaine étape** – Les scientifiques développent de meilleurs appareils d’exercice pour l’espace et la Terre afin de maintenir ces muscles actifs en continu. De nouvelles combinaisons et thérapies par vibration sont testées pour protéger les os et corriger la posture. Sur Terre, adopter des habitudes simples comme se tenir debout plus souvent, faire des exercices d’équilibre et renforcer le tronc peut soutenir la santé de la colonne vertébrale et ralentir le vieillissement.

**Phrase clé** – Les exercices et technologies conçus pour les astronautes aident les personnes sur Terre à réduire les douleurs dorsales, améliorer l’équilibre et garder leur force musculaire en vieillissant.
articles.personality_title_es: Cómo los ejercicios de astronautas reducen el dolor de espalda y ralentizan el envejecimiento
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los astronautas pierden fuerza muscular y masa ósea durante largas misiones espaciales debido a la microgravedad, que es como una versión acelerada del envejecimiento en la Tierra.

**Qué pasó** – Los científicos descubrieron que ciertos músculos profundos que sostienen la columna vertebral se debilitan en el espacio. Las agencias espaciales ahora usan ejercicios específicos y dispositivos nuevos para ayudar a los astronautas a mantener estos músculos fuertes durante las misiones y recuperarse al regresar. Algunas de estas técnicas, como los ejercicios de activación continua de baja intensidad (LICA) y los trajes que simulan la gravedad, se están adaptando para ayudar a personas en la Tierra con dolor de espalda, problemas de equilibrio y debilidad muscular.

**Impacto** – Esta investigación muestra que las formas en que los astronautas cuidan su salud muscular y ósea en el espacio también pueden mejorar problemas comunes en la vida diaria, como el dolor crónico de espalda y la pérdida muscular por envejecimiento. Los entrenamientos específicos fortalecen músculos que los ejercicios normales a menudo no trabajan. Dispositivos antigravedad diseñados para el espacio ahora ayudan a pacientes en rehabilitación y a personas mayores a mejorar su movilidad.

**Próximo paso** – Los científicos están desarrollando mejores máquinas de ejercicio para el espacio y la Tierra que mantengan los músculos centrales activos continuamente. Nuevos trajes y terapias de vibración se están probando para proteger los huesos y mejorar la postura. En la Tierra, adoptar hábitos simples como estar más tiempo de pie, hacer ejercicios de equilibrio y entrenar el core puede apoyar la salud de la columna y frenar los efectos del envejecimiento.

**Frase clave** – Los ejercicios y tecnologías diseñados para astronautas están ayudando a personas en la Tierra a reducir el dolor de espalda, mejorar el equilibrio y mantener la fuerza muscular con la edad.
